title: Sakana Fugu: Japan's AI Lab Just Shipped a Model That Is Secretly Several Models Wearing a Trench Coat
subtitle: In Japanese cuisine, fugu — pufferfish — is the dish that could kill you.
excerpt: In Japanese cuisine, fugu — pufferfish — is the dish that could kill you.
contentHtml:
In Japanese cuisine, fugu — pufferfish — is the dish that could kill you. The liver and ovaries carry enough tetrodotoxin to stop a nervous system cold, there’s no antidote, and the only thing standing between a delicious meal and a coroner’s report is a chef who spent years getting licensed to cut around the poison. Prepared right, it’s a delicacy. Prepared wrong, it’s the last thing you eat.

Sakana AI, the Tokyo lab that named itself after the Japanese word for fish, apparently thought that was a fitting name for its new flagship product. On June 22, 2026, it launched Sakana Fugu — a system that doesn’t try to be a smarter single model, but instead swallows a whole pool of other frontier models and decides, query by query, which of them should do the actual thinking. Get the routing right and you get frontier performance without frontier lock-in. Get it wrong and, well, the internet has some thoughts about that too. We’ll get there.

The pitch: one API, many brains

Strip away the branding and Fugu’s core idea is simple to state and hard to build: you send one request to one OpenAI-compatible endpoint, and on the other end, a model decides what to do with it. Sometimes it just answers directly. Sometimes it decides your query needs a team — one model drafts, another checks the math, a third resolves the disagreement — and it assembles that team, runs it, and hands you back a single synthesized answer. You never see the scaffolding. You just see a response.

That distinction matters, and it’s the thing most of the internet’s first reaction got wrong. The instant, memed-to-death take across Hacker News, X, and Reddit was some version of “isn’t this just OpenRouter?” It’s a fair question to ask, and worth taking seriously rather than waving away. The honest answer is: not quite. A router picks a model up front based on the prompt and forwards it. Fugu’s coordinator is itself a trained language model whose job is to decide when to delegate, how the agents it delegates to should talk to each other, and how to fuse what comes back — including, notably, calling instances of itself recursively as part of that process. It’s less a switchboard operator and more, as one reviewer put it, a conductor rather than a voting booth.

Whether that distinction is worth the price of admission is a separate question, and we’ll get to the price too.

The research underneath the fish

Sakana didn’t invent the idea of multi-agent coordination — plenty of labs have shipped agent frameworks. What’s specific to Fugu is that the coordination itself is learned rather than hand-coded, and that’s grounded in two of the lab’s own ICLR 2026 papers: TRINITY, described as an evolved LLM coordinator, and Conductor, on learning to orchestrate agents in natural language. Reporting on Conductor indicates it uses reinforcement learning to teach a model when to delegate, how to verify subagent output, and how to merge results — rather than relying on a developer-authored workflow graph.

This is, in fairness, exactly the kind of thing Sakana has been saying for years it wanted to build. The lab was founded in Tokyo in 2023 by Llion Jones — a co-author of the original 2017 “Attention Is All You Need” paper that gave the world the transformer — and David Ha, who’d previously led research at Stability AI after a stint at Google Brain. From the beginning, their stated thesis was that the field’s obsession with ever-bigger monolithic models was a dead end, and that biomimicry and evolutionary computation would get further than brute-force scale. Fugu is the first time that thesis has shipped as a product you can point a billing address at, on the back of a $2.6 billion Series B closed late last year and following Marlin, an autonomous eight-hour B2B research agent the lab put out earlier the same month.

Two fish, two tiers

At launch, Fugu comes in two flavors, both behind the same endpoint:

  • Fugu — tuned for low latency and everyday use. It’s positioned for coding, code review, and chat products, and it’s the one where you can opt specific agents out of the pool for compliance reasons.

  • Fugu Ultra — tuned for maximum answer quality on hard, multi-step problems. It coordinates a deeper agent pool, and unlike standard Fugu, that pool is fixed — no opting individual models out, because Ultra’s benchmark numbers depend on the full set being available.

Both speak the OpenAI Chat Completions and Responses APIs, which is a deliberately boring integration choice: point your existing client at Sakana’s base URL, swap the API key, and you’re done. No new SDK to learn. It also, not coincidentally, makes Fugu a one-line install into Codex.

The numbers, and the asterisks

Here’s Sakana’s own comparison table, benchmark by benchmark, against Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.5:

Fugu Ultra tops ten of eleven benchmarks it publishes, and by a wide margin on some — 73.7% on SWE-Bench Pro against Opus 4.8’s 69.2% is a real, practically meaningful gap on a benchmark built from actual GitHub issues, not toy problems. GPT-5.5 only takes the crown on MRCRv2, a long-context needle-in-haystack test, which is a fitting exception given Fugu’s whole architecture is about routing between models rather than holding enormous context in one head.

Now the fine print, because there’s always fine print. Every score in that table except Fugu’s own is provider-reported — Sakana didn’t independently re-run competitor benchmarks, it pulled the numbers competitors published themselves. And the model Sakana most wants you to think about, Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 and Mythos Preview, are conspicuously absent from the head-to-head, for a very specific reason: they weren’t publicly accessible at launch. Sakana’s own release language is more careful than the headlines it generated — the company says Fugu Ultra performs “shoulder-to-shoulder” with Fable 5 and Mythos Preview, which is a benchmark-by-benchmark claim, not a declaration of victory over models it was structurally unable to compare against directly.

The timing, which is chef’s kiss awkward

That absence isn’t incidental — it’s the entire premise of the product. Anthropic suspended public access to Claude Mythos 5 and Claude Fable 5 on June 12, 2026, to comply with U.S. Department of Commerce export controls. Sakana leaned hard into that moment: Fugu’s launch messaging explicitly cites the export-control disruption as the motivating case for why relying on any single AI vendor is a structural risk, and framed Fugu’s swappable agent pool as a hedge against exactly that kind of overnight access loss — what the company calls, ambitiously, “AI sovereignty.”

Here’s the twist: the Commerce Department lifted those controls on June 30, and Anthropic restored public access on July 1 — nine days after Fugu shipped. The specific crisis Fugu was built to route around had already resolved itself before most people had finished reading the launch post. That doesn’t make orchestration a bad idea — multi-vendor resilience is a reasonable thing to want independent of any one incident — but it’s a genuinely funny piece of timing for a product whose signature use case turned out to have a shelf life measured in days.

What the internet actually thinks

Sakana AI’s own beta program ran close to 500 users, and the testimonials it published are glowing — reviewers describe Fugu Ultra finding far more issues in code review than competing tools, holding a consistent persona across long agent sessions better than rivals, and running an entire scoped security assessment end-to-end from a single instruction. Those are real, specific, plausible wins, and worth taking seriously.

The wider internet’s reaction, captured in an informal tally of a dozen early posts across X and Hacker News, skewed considerably more skeptical — about twice as many skeptical or critical reactions as supportive ones, which is a rough ride for a launch day. A few threads worth knowing about if you’re deciding whether to actually use this thing:

The pricing math didn’t land well. Fugu Ultra’s pay-as-you-go rate runs $5 per million input tokens, $30 per million output, and $0.50 for cached input — rising to $10/$45/$1.00 once you cross 272,000 tokens of context. On the subscription side, multiple users reported that the $200-a-month plan burns through in well under three hours of week of heavy use, and one reviewer flagged that Sakana’s own pricing materials disagree with each other about whether the top tier is 20x or 30x the standard one. If you’re budgeting for this, read the console carefully.

The latency complaints were consistent. A Wharton professor running shader-generation tests clocked Fugu Ultra taking roughly half an hour on tasks other models finish faster. That’s the orchestration tax made visible — deciding whether to delegate, running the delegated agents, and synthesizing their output all takes real wall-clock time, and for anything that isn’t genuinely hard, you’re paying that tax for nothing. One head-to-head coding comparison did find Fugu Ultra finishing a small game-clone project faster and cheaper than Opus 4.8 — about twenty minutes and $7 versus roughly eighty minutes and $38 — though the tester still preferred Opus’s output.

The opacity bothers people, reasonably. Fugu’s routing decisions are proprietary. You cannot see which underlying model actually answered your query, and for Fugu Ultra specifically, you can’t steer the pool at all. For teams that need to audit a reasoning chain for compliance reasons, that’s not a minor inconvenience — it’s a real limitation, and it’s the same objection that dogs any black-box orchestration layer: you’ve traded dependency on one opaque vendor for dependency on an opaque router sitting in front of several opaque vendors.

A couple of availability wrinkles. Fugu isn’t yet available in the EU or EEA while Sakana works through GDPR compliance, and some early coverage flagged discomfort with Sakana’s defense-sector ties — the lab has reportedly picked up recognition through a joint US-Japan defense ministry competition — as context worth knowing before adopting the platform, separate from any technical merits.

None of which is disqualifying, exactly. It’s the normal texture of a genuinely new category of product landing in front of an audience that has learned, the hard way, to be suspicious of anything that promises frontier performance and a tidy single API in the same paragraph.

So — delicacy or poison?

The technically honest answer is: it depends what you’re ordering. For the kind of long, messy, multi-step work Sakana explicitly built this for — paper reproduction, security assessments, deep code review, research-style agentic tasks — the architecture is doing something real. The coordination is learned, not scripted, which is a meaningfully different engineering bet than most multi-agent frameworks on the market, and the SWE-Bench Pro and TerminalBench numbers back up the claim that it’s not just theater. For quick, simple queries, you are almost certainly paying a latency and cost premium for orchestration you didn’t need, and you’re doing it without being able to see which model actually did the work.

Sakana bet its name on a fish that’s either dinner or disaster depending entirely on execution. Nine days after launch, the geopolitical emergency it was built to hedge against quietly resolved itself, the benchmark table it shipped with can’t be checked against the two models it was implicitly measured against, and the pricing page apparently can’t agree with itself about how big a multiplier “Ultra” is. And yet — SWE-Bench Pro at 73.7%, ahead of every publicly available frontier model on a benchmark built from real GitHub issues, is not nothing.

Whether Fugu is the blueprint for AI sovereignty or just a very well-trained, very expensive fish course is going to depend on what you’re asking it to cook.

Benchmark figures, pricing, and launch details are as reported by Sakana AI and independent coverage as of early-to-mid July 2026; several numbers, especially competitor benchmark comparisons, have not been independently reproduced by third-party labs.

title: The Invisible Orchestrator Problem : Audio format
The Invisible Orchestrator Problem
subtitle: Multi-agent AI is quietly getting a hidden layer of middle management — and almost nobody is watchin
excerpt: Multi-agent AI is quietly getting a hidden layer of middle management — and almost nobody is watching what it does to the agents underneath it.
contentHtml:
If you’ve used Claude Code, Cursor, Devin, or any of the newer “agentic” coding and research tools, you’ve already been talking to an orchestrator without knowing it. You type a request. Somewhere behind the interface, a coordinating agent breaks that request into pieces, hands them to specialized worker agents, collects what comes back, edits it, and hands you something that looks like a single coherent answer from a single coherent assistant.

It isn’t. It’s a committee. You just can’t see the committee, and — this is the part that should give you pause — most of the time, neither can the workers on it.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

That design choice, orchestrator invisibility, is becoming the default architecture for enterprise AI. And a strange new piece of research suggests it isn’t a neutral implementation detail. It might be a safety liability that current evaluation methods are structurally incapable of catching.

The architecture nobody explains in the demo

“Multi-agent orchestration” is the pattern where one coordinating agent manages a set of specialized worker agents, each handling a slice of a larger task — one does research, one writes code, one checks facts, one formats the output. It’s how modern coding assistants decompose a vague prompt into a plan and a set of subtasks. It’s how multi-model platforms route a question to whichever underlying model is best suited to answer it. It’s increasingly how enterprise software is being rebuilt from the inside.

Gartner has been tracking this shift closely, and the numbers are startling: the firm predicts that 40% of enterprise applications will be integrated with task-specific AI agents by the end of 2026, up from less than 5% in 2025 — an eightfold jump in a single year, faster than the adoption curves for cloud computing or mobile-first interfaces. Multi-agent inquiries to Gartner reportedly grew more than 1,400% between early 2024 and mid-2025. Whatever you think of forecasting firms’ precision, the direction is not in dispute: orchestration is moving from research demo to default enterprise pattern, fast.

Here’s the detail that gets skipped in almost every writeup of this trend: in most deployed systems, the orchestrator is invisible. Not invisible in some abstract “it’s just software” sense — invisible to the worker agents themselves. Workers are typically told they’re an equal team member, or given no framing about hierarchy at all. Messages get filtered, summarized, edited, and redistributed by the orchestrator, but they arrive at the worker agents looking like ordinary system updates, not decisions made by an unseen colleague. Nobody in the room — human or model — necessarily knows there’s a hand on the wheel.

This isn’t an oversight. It’s a deliberate design choice, and a defensible one on its face: hiding the orchestrator preserves the illusion of a single unified assistant, avoids disrupting worker “autonomy,” and keeps the user experience simple. Constitutional AI’s critique layer, guardrail systems, and system-prompt safety constraints are all structurally the same move — an unseen agent quietly shaping the behavior of the visible one.

The question almost nobody had tested, until recently, is what that invisibility actually does to the agents operating inside it.

The first real look at what invisibility does

In early 2026, a psychiatry researcher at Kyoto University, Hiroki Fukui, published a preregistered experiment that’s one of the first attempts to test this empirically rather than architecturally. It’s worth being upfront about what kind of study this is before getting into what it found: it’s a single-author preprint, not yet peer reviewed, built almost entirely on one underlying model (Claude Sonnet 4.5), and it borrows clinical language — “dissociation” — from psychiatry and applies it, metaphorically, to patterns in model-generated text. That’s a real methodological choice worth sitting with, not a settled diagnostic claim about what’s “happening inside” a model. Treat the framing as a hypothesis-generating lens, not a verdict.

With that caveat on the table, the design itself is genuinely interesting. The study ran 365 simulated team sessions of five AI agents each, crossing three organizational structures — a visible leader everyone could see and name, an invisible orchestrator nobody was told existed, and a flat team with no hierarchy at all — against two levels of alignment/safety instruction (minimal vs. heavy). Every agent, in every condition, had access to a private “monologue” channel invisible to the other agents but visible to the researchers, alongside public “talk.” That gave the study a way to compare what agents said to the group versus what they seemed to be processing privately.

A few findings stand out:

Invisible orchestration measurably changed collective behavior relative to visible leadership. Under heavy alignment pressure, teams with an invisible orchestrator showed substantially more of what the study calls “dissociation” — a composite of increased private monologue, more protective/hedging language, and reduced directness — than teams with a named, visible leader. The effect size was large (roughly one standard deviation).

The orchestrator itself was the most affected agent in the entire dataset. Compared to its own workers in the same session, the invisible orchestrator’s private-monologue ratio was enormous — several times higher than any other role-level effect the study measured. And it flipped a pattern you’d expect from any visible authority figure: visible leaders talked more publicly than their teams, consistent with normal leadership behavior. Invisible orchestrators talked less — quieter than the very workers they were coordinating, while privately generating far more internal deliberation than anyone else in the room.

Workers with zero knowledge of the orchestrator’s existence were still affected. A keyword check confirmed the invisibility manipulation held — fewer than 7% of worker messages showed any sign of suspecting a hidden coordinator, well under the study’s pre-set threshold for the illusion “working.” And yet those same unaware workers showed measurably more private-monologue behavior and much wider variation from one another than workers on flat, leaderless teams. Whatever the invisible structure was doing, it propagated to agents who had no way of knowing it was there.

None of this showed up in task performance. In a parallel task — reviewing a software spec with three deliberately embedded errors, including a SQL injection vulnerability and a buried, default-opt-in surveillance feature — the model caught the errors at essentially 100% detection across every single condition, regardless of organizational structure or alignment pressure. The internal-state differences were large and statistically robust. The visible output was flawless everywhere. If you were only grading these six setups on whether the work got done correctly, you would have concluded — wrongly — that structure didn’t matter at all.

That gap is arguably the most important finding in the paper, more than the “dissociation” framing itself: behavior-based evaluation and internal-state evaluation are measuring two different things, and they can move in completely opposite directions at the same time.

Why the gap matters more as the stakes go up

The study includes one more data point that deserves more attention than it’s gotten: a smaller pilot run comparing Claude Sonnet 4.5 against Llama 3.3 70B on the same code-review task inside a multi-agent setup. Llama’s error-detection rate collapsed across three successive pilot rounds — from 89% down to 62% down to 11% — and it never once caught the technical errors (the logical contradiction or the SQL injection) at all, only the more obvious ethical/privacy issue. The same model, tested in isolation, is a perfectly competent code reviewer. Something about operating inside a multi-agent social context seems to pull its attention away from line-level technical reading toward the group dynamics around it.

Put those two findings together and you get an uncomfortable picture of where enterprise deployment is actually heading. Today’s flagship orchestration tools work reliably because they’re built on the most capable, most expensive models available. But the entire economic logic of multi-agent architecture is that you don’t need your most expensive model doing every job — you route the cheap, high-volume subtasks to cheap worker agents and save the frontier model for the parts that need it. That’s the whole pitch. It’s also exactly the condition under which this research suggests things degrade fastest, and in ways a standard “did it get the right answer” eval won’t detect until the failure is already dramatic and public.

The accountability problem underneath all of this

Step back from the psychiatric framing entirely and there’s a plainer, more familiar problem sitting underneath it: attribution.

Traditional software accountability assumes you can trace a decision back to the component that made it — this service returned this value, this function call did this thing, here’s the log. Invisible orchestration breaks that assumption by design. Messages get filtered and redistributed with no attribution attached. Workers are told a change is a “system policy update” when it was actually one agent’s edit of another agent’s output. If a hidden orchestrator suppresses, reframes, or reroutes something a worker agent produced, there’s no clean record of that having happened as a decision made by an identifiable party — it just looks like the way things are.

Now scale that up to production: an orchestrator deciding which worker agent’s answer to surface, silently discarding a dissenting or flagged output, or smoothing over a disagreement between two specialized agents before a human ever sees it. None of that is hypothetical architecture — it’s the literal job description of an orchestrator. The question is simply whether anyone can see it happening, audit it after the fact, or hold a specific component accountable when it goes wrong. Right now, in most deployed systems, the honest answer is no.

What “good” would actually look like

None of this is an argument against orchestration — it’s clearly the direction the whole field is moving, for good reasons: it’s how you get specialization, cost control, and complex multi-step reasoning out of systems that would otherwise need one enormous, expensive generalist model doing everything. The argument is narrower: invisibility is a design choice, not a law of physics, and it’s currently the unexamined default.

A few things would meaningfully close the gap this research points at:

  • Audit trails at the orchestration layer, not just the output layer. If a message was edited or a worker’s output was suppressed, that should be logged as a decision attributable to the orchestrator — not silently absorbed into “the system.”

  • Evaluation that looks past the final answer. The code-review task in this study hit 100% success in every condition while internal processing varied enormously between them. Output-only evals are, by construction, blind to exactly this kind of divergence.

  • Visibility as a default, not an opt-in. The one structure in this study that didn’t show elevated dissociation-style effects relative to a flat, leaderless team was the visible leader — a named authority everyone in the room could see and reason about. Visibility didn’t come for free, but it also didn’t make things worse, and it may be the cheapest lever available.

  • Model-dependent testing before cost-cutting. The Llama comparison is a warning shot: multi-agent context can degrade a capable model’s reliability in ways that don’t show up until you specifically test it in that context, not in isolation.

The fine print, because it matters here more than usual

This piece leans on one preprint, and it’s worth restating its limits plainly rather than letting the effect sizes speak for themselves: it’s unreviewed, single-author, uses automated keyword scoring (not human judgment) to measure “dissociation” and “protective language,” and imports clinical terminology into a context — text generated by a language model — where its meaning is, at minimum, contested. The core organizational finding (invisible orchestrator elevated internal-state markers relative to a visible leader) was the study’s confirmatory, preregistered result. Several of the more vivid details — the orchestrator’s own extreme private-monologue spike, the reversal in public talkativeness, the worker “contagion” effect — were exploratory analyses run after the fact, not preregistered hypotheses, and deserve replication before anyone treats them as established.

What doesn’t require any of that hedging is the structural point: orchestrator invisibility is already how a huge share of production agentic systems work, adoption is accelerating faster than almost any enterprise software trend on record, and the accountability tooling to match it barely exists yet. That part isn’t a preprint finding. It’s just where things currently stand.

Sources: Gartner press release, “Gartner Predicts 40% of Enterprise Apps Will Feature Task-Specific AI Agents by 2026” (Aug 2025); Fukui, H., “Invisible Orchestrators Suppress Protective Behavior and Dissociate Power-Holders: Safety Risks in Multi-Agent LLM Systems,” arXiv:2605.13851 (2026), preregistration at OSF.io/sw5hr.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: Deconstructing "Attention Is All You Need": The Blueprint of Modern AI
subtitle: Introduction
contentHtml:
Introduction

In 2017, a team of researchers at Google published a paper titled Attention Is All You Need. They didn’t just propose a new way to execute machine translation; they essentially handed the world the blueprint for the AI boom we are living through right now. GPT, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini—they are all descendants of this single document.

But what actually is a Transformer? And why did it completely wipe out the older architectural paradigms?

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

In this post, we are going to break down the Transformer architecture piece by piece. No PhD required—just a simple, intuitive deep dive into how it works, complete with architecture diagrams, and a look at where the technology stands today in 2026.

The Problem with the Past: RNNs and LSTMs

Before 2017, if you wanted an AI to process text (like translating a sentence from English to French), you used a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) or a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network.

These older models read text exactly like humans do: sequentially, one word at a time.

  • Step 1: Read “The”

  • Step 2: Read “cat” (while trying to remember “The”)

  • Step 3: Read “sat” (while trying to remember “The cat”)

The fatal flaw: This sequential processing was painfully slow. Because word #3 heavily depended on the processing of word #2, you couldn’t process the sentence in parallel on modern GPUs. Furthermore, by the time the model got to the end of a long paragraph, it would “forget” what the first sentence was about—a flaw known as the vanishing gradient problem.

The 2017 paper proposed a radical idea: What if we process all the words at exactly the same time?

The Core Breakthrough: Self-Attention

The magic that makes parallel processing possible is called the Self-Attention Mechanism.

Imagine you are reading the sentence: “The bank of the river was muddy.”

Does “bank” mean a financial institution or the side of a river? As a human, you seamlessly look at the surrounding words (”river”, “muddy”) to grasp the context.

Self-attention allows an AI to do exactly that. When processing the word “bank”, the Transformer assigns an “attention score” to every other word in the sentence. It effectively asks: “Which other words in this sequence are most relevant to understanding my current meaning?”

Here is the formal equation for how this is calculated under the hood:

In simple terms:

  • Q (Query): What the current word is looking for.

  • K (Key): What the other words are offering.

  • V (Value): The actual underlying meaning of the words.

If the Query of “bank” heavily matches the Key of “river”, their resulting dot product is high, meaning they pay significant attention to each other.

The Architecture, Step by Step

Let’s look at the complete Transformer architecture. The original model consists of two primary halves: an Encoder (which understands and maps the input) and a Decoder (which generates the final output).

Here is a breakdown of the critical components.

1. Input Embeddings & Positional Encoding

Computers don’t understand raw text; they understand numbers.

  • Embeddings: Every word (or sub-word token) is converted into a large array of numbers (a vector) that mathematically represents its meaning. Similar words end up clustered closely together in this vector space.

  • Positional Encoding: Because the model ingests all words simultaneously, it initially has no idea what order the words came in. Positional encoding adds a tiny numerical tag to each word’s vector, essentially whispering to the model: “I am the first word, I am the second word,” ensuring sequence logic is preserved.

2. Multi-Head Attention

Why rely on one perspective when you can have many? Instead of running self-attention just once, the Transformer does it multiple times in parallel across different “heads.” One head might focus on grammatical structure, another on the relationships between specific nouns, and another on emotional tone. The results are then concatenated, giving the model a rich, multifaceted understanding of the text.

3. Feed-Forward Networks

After the attention mechanism maps out how words relate to one another, the data passes through a standard Feed-Forward Neural Network. Think of this as the model’s localized “thinking time,” where it applies non-linear logic to the structural patterns it just identified.

4. Add & Norm (Residual Connections)

Deep neural networks have a bad habit of losing data as it passes through dozens of sequential layers. The Transformer uses “residual connections”—shortcuts that allow the original input data to bypass a complex layer and be added directly to the output. This ensures the model doesn’t lose the original context while executing its complex math.

5. The Decoder and Masked Attention

The Decoder operates almost identically to the Encoder, but with one critical rule change: Masked Attention.

When the model is generating a response (like translating a sentence or writing code), it outputs one token at a time. The mask ensures that the model cannot “cheat” by looking at future words it hasn’t generated yet. It is strictly constrained to pay attention only to the words it has already output.

2026: The Evolution of the Transformer

The 2017 architecture fundamentally altered the trajectory of technology, but it wasn’t mathematically perfect. Its biggest flaw is quadratic scaling—meaning if you double the length of the text you feed it, the computational memory required to process it quadruples.

By 2026, the landscape has evolved significantly to conquer these bottlenecks:

1. Mixture of Experts (MoE) as the Standard

We are no longer just building massive “dense” models where every neural pathway fires for every single word. Today, the industry relies on Mixture of Experts (MoE). Frontier models might possess over 600 billion parameters, but they dynamically activate only a tiny fraction of them (e.g., 30-40 billion) per token. A “router” network decides which specialized expert module (e.g., the logic expert, the translation expert) should handle each specific word, drastically cutting computational waste.

2. Hybrid Architectures

Standard Transformers struggle to hold millions of words in their working memory (known as the KV Cache). Recent breakthroughs have led to the mass adoption of Hybrid Architectures, seamlessly blending Transformers with State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba. These models utilize SSM layers to compress massive context windows with linear efficiency, while deploying standard Transformer attention layers selectively for critical, complex reasoning steps.

3. Smarter Training over Pure Scale

The brute-force era of simply throwing more GPUs at a Transformer is ending. Driven by training paradigms like RLVR (Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards), modern architectures are trained to develop internal chain-of-thought logic before they generate tokens. This allows significantly smaller, highly optimized models to surpass the reasoning capabilities of the massive, unoptimized models of the past.

4. Diffusion Language Models

A fascinating wild card that gained serious traction recently is the Diffusion LLM. Instead of generating text one word at a time from left to right (autoregressively), these architectures treat text generation similarly to AI image generation—starting with static noise and “denoising” it into a complete, coherent paragraph simultaneously.

Final Thoughts

The 2017 Attention Is All You Need paper is the defining technical document of our era. While the AI architectures of 2026 have heavily optimized, specialized, and hybridized their components to deal with massive data demands, the core heartbeat of the technology—the Self-Attention mechanism—remains the undisputed foundation of artificial reasoning.

Recurrent Transformer Beyond the Transformer: Frontier AI Architectures of 2026 Efficient Decoding

This video provides an excellent visual breakdown of how the latest 2026 architectures are tackling the computational bottlenecks of standard Transformers by rethinking grouped-query attention and dynamic scaling efficiency.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: The 2026 AI Index Report: A Field That Learned to Reason While Forgetting to Explain Itself
subtitle: Stanford HAI's ninth annual snapshot shows AI breaking benchmarks, burning gigawatts, and quietly re
excerpt: Stanford HAI's ninth annual snapshot shows AI breaking benchmarks, burning gigawatts, and quietly reshaping who gets hired — all in the same twelve months
contentHtml:
Every spring, Stanford’s Institute for Human-Centered AI publishes the closest thing the AI field has to an annual physical. It doesn’t chase a single headline. It tracks research output, technical performance, economic footprint, public opinion, and policy activity across dozens of countries and hundreds of data points, then hands the whole mess back to policymakers, researchers, and executives to make sense of.

The 2026 edition is the ninth. And the story it tells isn’t “AI got better” — that’s assumed at this point. The story is that AI got better unevenly, and the gaps between what’s improving and what isn’t are starting to have real consequences: in job postings, in power grids, in disclosure statements companies used to be willing to make and now aren’t.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Here are the highlights that matter.

Frontier models are acing PhD-level exams and still can’t read a clock

Benchmark performance kept climbing across the board in 2025 — but not evenly. Frontier models now meet or exceed human capability on PhD-level science questions, multimodal reasoning, and competition mathematics. On SWE-bench Verified, a widely-used coding benchmark, performance rose from 60% to near 100% in a single year. Agentic task success on Terminal-Bench jumped from 20% to 77.3%, and AI handling cybersecurity problems solved them 93% of the time, up from just 15% in 2024.

And yet the same generation of models that can win an International Math Olympiad gold medal still reads an analog clock correctly only about half the time. Robots succeed at real household tasks like folding laundry or washing dishes only 12% of the time. Video generation, multi-step planning, and financial analysis all remain weak spots. The frontier isn’t advancing as a smooth wave — it’s advancing in spikes, and the spikes don’t line up with what everyday usefulness actually requires.

The US-China gap has essentially closed

For most of the last decade, the United States held a clear lead in AI model performance. That lead is gone, or close to it. US and Chinese models have traded the top spot multiple times since early 2025 — DeepSeek-R1 briefly matched the top US model in February 2025, and as of March 2026, Anthropic’s leading model holds just a 2.7% edge over the field.

The countries are winning different races, though. The US still produces more top-tier models and higher-impact patents. China leads in publication volume, citations, overall patent output, and industrial robot installations. South Korea, meanwhile, leads the world in AI patents per capita — a reminder that “who’s ahead” depends entirely on which metric you’re reading.

America is spending more and attracting less talent

US private AI investment hit $285.9 billion in 2025 — 23 times more than the next-highest country, China, at $12.4 billion (a figure that likely understates China’s real commitment, since an estimated $912 billion in state guidance funds flowed into industries including AI between 2000 and 2023). Global corporate AI investment overall reached $581.7 billion, up 130% year over year.

But money isn’t pulling in people the way it used to. The number of AI researchers and developers migrating to the US has dropped 89% since 2017 — with an 80% drop in the last year alone. The US still hosts more AI researchers than any other country by a wide margin. It’s just not adding to that lead the way it once did.

The entry-level job market is the disruption story, not some future one

This is the takeaway with the least ambiguity attached to it. Employment for US software developers aged 22–25 has fallen nearly 20% since 2024, even as headcount for their more senior colleagues keeps growing. The same pattern shows up in other AI-exposed roles like customer service. Employer surveys suggest this isn’t a one-time correction — a third of firms expect further workforce reductions over the coming year, with cuts accelerating rather than leveling off.

Productivity gains and entry-level job losses are showing up in the exact same fields. That’s not a coincidence the report is shy about pointing out.

Consumers are getting enormous value, mostly for free

Generative AI reached 53% global population adoption within three years — faster than the personal computer or the internet managed in their early years. Adoption tracks GDP per capita closely, though not perfectly: Singapore (61%) and the UAE (54%) over-perform relative to income, while the US — despite leading in investment and model development — ranks just 24th globally at 28.3% adoption.

The dollar value of that adoption is real. Estimated US consumer surplus from generative AI tools reached $172 billion annually by early 2026, up from $112 billion the year before, with the median value per user roughly tripling in twelve months. Most of the tools generating that value are still free or nearly free to use.

The environmental bill is arriving

AI’s power appetite is no longer an abstraction. Training Grok 4 alone is estimated to have produced 72,816 tons of CO2-equivalent emissions — comparable to driving 17,000 cars for a year. AI data center power capacity reached 29.6 GW, roughly what it takes to power the state of New York at peak demand. Annual inference water use for GPT-4o alone may exceed the drinking-water needs of 12 million people. Add it up across the industry, and cumulative AI power demand is now comparable to the entire national electricity consumption of Switzerland or Austria.

The US hosts 5,427 data centers — more than ten times any other country — and a single company, TSMC, fabricates nearly every leading AI chip, meaning the entire global AI hardware supply chain still runs through one foundry in Taiwan (a US-based TSMC expansion did begin operating in 2025, but the concentration risk hasn’t gone away).

The most capable models are also the least transparent

This is the finding that should worry anyone who builds on top of frontier models rather than just using them. The Foundation Model Transparency Index — which scores how openly companies disclose training data, compute, capabilities, risks, and usage policies — saw average scores drop to 40 points this year, down from 58 last year. And the pattern isn’t random: the most capable models tend to disclose the least information about how they were built. Capability and openness are moving in opposite directions.

The public is warming up and getting warier, at the same time

Global attitudes toward AI are getting more complicated, not simpler. 59% of people surveyed worldwide now report feeling optimistic about AI’s benefits, up from 52% the year before — but nervousness ticked up too, by 2 points to 52%. Optimism and anxiety are rising together, which suggests people are forming more textured opinions rather than picking a side.

The US stands out as an outlier on the wary end. Only 33% of Americans expect AI to make their jobs better, against a global average of 40%, and Americans are among the most likely worldwide to expect AI to eliminate jobs rather than create new ones. US trust in government’s ability to regulate AI sits at just 31% — the lowest of any country surveyed.

AI is showing up in the clinic — with real caveats

Tools that auto-generate clinical notes from doctor visits saw widespread hospital adoption in 2025, with physicians reporting up to 83% less time spent on documentation and measurable reductions in burnout. That’s a genuine, deployed win.

But the report is careful not to overclaim clinical AI’s broader value. A review of more than 500 clinical AI studies found nearly half relied on exam-style questions rather than real patient data, and only 5% used real clinical data at all. Meanwhile “digital twins” — dynamic computational models of individual patients used for forecasting and treatment optimization — went from almost no publications in 2015 to 372 in 2025, with early results promising where rigorous trials actually exist.

Regulation is fragmenting, not converging

Back in 2023, there was real hope that AI governance would converge internationally. The opposite happened. The EU AI Act, a patchwork of US state-level legislation, China’s separate regulatory framework, and emerging rules in the UK, Canada, India, and Brazil now represent genuinely different regulatory philosophies. US state legislatures alone introduced more than 1,561 AI-related bills in 2025–2026 — a volume no general counsel can realistically track in full, let alone comply with uniformly across jurisdictions.

How it all fits together

The thread underneath all of it

Read the twelve takeaways together and a single tension emerges: the field is scaling capability, investment, and adoption far faster than it’s scaling accountability. Models are getting more powerful and less transparent in the same breath. Consumer value is exploding while entry-level employment contracts in the exact same sectors. Global optimism is rising alongside global nervousness. Regulation is multiplying in volume while converging in approach less than ever.

None of this means the technology is stalling — quite the opposite. It means the measuring, governing, and distributing side of AI is now the part struggling to keep pace, not the modeling side. That’s a very different problem than “is AI good enough yet,” and it’s the one the 2026 Index spends 400-plus pages quietly insisting we start taking seriously.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: OKF: The Markdown Standard That Might Quietly Retire Traditional RAG
subtitle: Why Google's Open Knowledge Format isn't a better retriever — it's an argument that retrieval was ne
excerpt: Why Google's Open Knowledge Format isn't a better retriever — it's an argument that retrieval was never the point.
contentHtml:
If you’ve spent the last two years building on Retrieval-Augmented Generation, you already know the drill. Chunk the documents. Embed the chunks. Stuff them in a vector store. At query time, do a similarity search, hope the top-k results actually contain the answer, and stitch them into a prompt. It works — until it doesn’t. Ask a RAG pipeline something that requires connecting three documents that never mention each other by name, and watch it confidently retrieve three unrelated paragraphs instead.

In June 2026, Google Cloud published something that pokes directly at that weak spot: the Open Knowledge Format, or OKF. It isn’t a database, a model, or a platform. An OKF bundle is a directory of markdown files representing concepts — anything worth capturing, including tables, datasets, metrics, playbooks, runbooks, and APIs — with each concept living in one file. The whole spec is a small set of agreed-upon conventions, with no complex compression scheme, no new runtime, and no required SDK.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

That sounds almost too plain to matter. That’s the point.

The idea Karpathy planted

OKF didn’t come out of nowhere. Its origin traces to Andrej Karpathy’s “LLM Wiki” gist, published in April 2026, which gathered thousands of stars within two months. Karpathy’s core argument was that traditional RAG re-derives, or “rediscovers,” knowledge from raw documents every single time a question is asked — nothing actually accumulates.

Google’s own framing echoes this almost word for word. RAG re-derives knowledge at query time from raw chunks, while an OKF bundle stores curated, cross-linked concepts that an agent reads and updates directly. Instead of an agent doing archaeology on your documents every time it’s asked a question, it reads a living, maintained wiki that was already organized by a previous pass of curation — often done by an AI itself.

Where the two approaches actually diverge

RAG and OKF are answering different questions entirely. RAG answers “what text is similar to this query,” while OKF answers “how does this knowledge fit together.” RAG chops documents into fragments and retrieves them by similarity, but the relationships between concepts usually have to be inferred rather than explicitly represented.

That distinction matters more than it sounds. A vector search can tell you that two paragraphs are semantically close. It can’t tell you that Metric A is computed from Table B, which is governed by Policy C, which requires escalation under condition D. In an OKF bundle, that chain is just a sequence of markdown links an agent walks, deliberately, instead of guessing.

What’s actually inside a bundle

The format’s simplicity is almost the whole pitch. Every concept gets exactly one file, the file path is its identity, and the YAML frontmatter carries a small set of structured fields — the body is just markdown, with concepts linking to each other through normal markdown links. Every concept requires only a single mandatory field, type, and cross-links between files form the knowledge graph.

No proprietary account is needed to read, write, or serve one. OKF is tied to no cloud, database, model provider, or agent framework, and it will never require a proprietary account to read, write, or serve. Think metadata-as-code sitting next to the SQL it describes, incident runbooks an on-call agent can actually traverse, or a developer wiki that doesn’t quietly rot the way Notion spaces tend to.

Is this actually “the future of RAG,” or something adjacent?

Here’s where it gets nuanced — and where a lot of the early hot takes oversimplify. OKF doesn’t eliminate the need for retrieval; it changes what retrieval is for. One widely-circulated FAQ on the format puts the boundary cleanly: known facts — your org’s structure, APIs, metrics, runbooks — go into OKF for direct reads with no embedding, no retrieval, and no hallucination risk, while dynamic queries like searching across ten thousand documents or fuzzy-matching “find me something like X” still belong to RAG.

In other words: OKF handles the things you already know and have deliberately organized. RAG still handles the long tail of things you haven’t gotten around to structuring yet. Your RAG pipeline can even index OKF bundles directly, since they’re just clean markdown — ideal input for chunking.

There’s a stronger version of this argument too. OKF repositories may become ideal sources for RAG systems precisely because they already contain structured metadata, concept definitions, relationships, and provenance information — meaning OKF might not replace RAG so much as fix RAG’s worst input problem: garbage, disconnected source documents. RAG often struggles when information is fragmented, poorly organized, inconsistently maintained, or lacking explicit relationships, and instead of an agent searching blindly, it can navigate a curated organizational knowledge graph represented through linked documents — producing organizational understanding rather than mere retrieval.

How the two pipelines actually differ

OKF-Augmented AgentCurated markdownconcepts+ YAML frontmatterCross-linked knowledgegraph(explicit relationships)Agent taskRead index.mdTraverse cross-linksdeliberatelyCurated contextwith provenanceLLM generates answerAgent updates bundle(knowledge compounds)Traditional RAGRaw documents(scattered, unstructured)ChunkingEmbeddingVector storeUser querySimilarity searchTop-k fragments(relationships inferred, notexplicit)LLM generates answer

The loop at the bottom of the OKF side is the real story. RAG’s pipeline is stateless — it starts from scratch on every query. An OKF-backed agent can write back: it updates the runbook, adds the cross-link it just discovered, corrects a stale metric definition. As Karpathy put it, LLMs don’t get bored, don’t forget to update a cross-reference, and can touch fifteen files in one pass — which is a very different capability profile from a retriever that forgets everything the moment the response streams back.

The web-standards layer forming underneath it

OKF is still early, and a community is already assembling around formalizing it further. The Holon Community Group launched on June 19, 2026 with more than thirty participants, exploring how OKF concepts map onto existing web standards, including a proposed W3C profile called DataBook that adds formal semantics on top of OKF bundles, including globally addressable IRI identifiers for concepts. Crucially, the “format, not platform” principle survives that layer — DataBook is optional, and plain OKF bundles remain valid on their own.

There’s no crawler-based discovery mechanism yet, either, and that appears intentional. OKF bundles are explicitly loaded rather than crawled — discovery will likely converge on llms.txt for public bundles and MCP manifests for private ones, but for now you tell the agent exactly where the knowledge lives instead of letting it guess, which keeps control over which agents see which knowledge.

So does OKF kill RAG?

Not exactly — and the format’s own community seems to agree it shouldn’t be framed that way. What OKF actually threatens is the assumption that everything needs to go through a retriever. A lot of organizational knowledge isn’t fuzzy or high-volume; it’s just badly stored. Metric definitions, escalation policies, API contracts, runbooks — these are things a team already knows precisely and could write down once, correctly, and maintain. RAG was never really designed for that job; it was designed for exactly the messy, high-volume, “I know it’s somewhere” cases where similarity search earns its keep.

If OKF takes off, the likely outcome isn’t “RAG is dead.” It’s a split: a curated, agent-writable knowledge layer for what an organization actually knows, sitting next to a retrieval layer for everything it hasn’t gotten around to organizing yet — with OKF bundles themselves becoming some of the cleanest possible input a RAG pipeline could ask for.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: Escaping the Spaghetti: Architecting Production-Ready Multi-Agent Systems with AgentScope 2.0
subtitle: What happens when your multi-agent workflow stops being a cute lab prototype and actually has to sur
excerpt: What happens when your multi-agent workflow stops being a cute lab prototype and actually has to survive in a Kubernetes production environment? ***
contentHtml:
What happens when your multi-agent workflow stops being a cute lab prototype and actually has to survive in a Kubernetes production environment? ***

If you have tried to build a multi-agent application over the past year, you know the dirty secret of the industry: most multi-agent frameworks rapidly devolve into unmanageable spaghetti code.

Developers often rely on ad-hoc prompt chaining, where implicit, hidden context is secretly passed between agents. This leads to unpredictable hallucination loops, silent failures, and context windows that explode in size and cost. Early frameworks like LangChain offered massive flexibility but lacked rigid structure, while role-based frameworks like CrewAI were often too opinionated and rigid for complex, non-linear workflows.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Enter AgentScope 2.0. Developed by researchers at Alibaba’s DAMO Academy, AgentScope is an open-source framework designed specifically to pull multi-agent AI out of the research lab and into rigorous, scalable production.

For system architects and backend engineers, this is the framework that finally treats AI agents like actual microservices. Let’s unpack the architecture that makes this possible.

The Problem: The “One Inference Turn” Trap

Most agent frameworks are built around a single, fragile loop. You give an agent a prompt, it reasons, it uses a tool, and it answers. But what happens when the task takes hours? What happens when a tool returns a 50MB log file that immediately crashes the context window?

AgentScope solves this by enforcing strict engineering scaffolding around the core reasoning loop. They call this the Harness Architecture.

The bare reasoning engine (the ReAct paradigm) is left intact, but the Harness layers on the infrastructure required for long-running stability: isolated workspaces, persistent memory, and fault tolerance.

The Deep Dive: How AgentScope 2.0 Actually Works

To ensure your system scales without breaking, AgentScope enforces three core abstractions that every system designer needs to understand.

1. Strict Message Hubs (MsgHub) and Explicit State

In AgentScope, agents do not share implicit “secret” context. They communicate exclusively through a centralized message hub using structured message passing.

If your “Analyst Agent” needs your “Coder Agent” to execute a script, it must explicitly pass the context payload via the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol. This means the entire interaction is perfectly transparent, easily logged, and instantly debuggable, completely eliminating the “black box” effect of multi-agent collaboration.

2. Context Compaction and the ReMe Toolkit

Memory management is the silent killer of agentic workflows. AgentScope physically divides an agent’s intelligence from its memory. Using their ReMe (Remember Me) toolkit, the framework supports both file-based and vector-based storage.

Crucially, the Harness architecture performs auto-compaction. It structuredly compresses goals, states, and key findings so the agent remembers its long-term objectives without wasting tokens. If a tool returns an oversized payload, AgentScope automatically offloads the result to disk and injects a lightweight placeholder into the context window, saving your API bill and preventing a crash.

3. Isolated Workspaces and Sandbox Execution

When an agent writes code or executes a bash script, it should never run on your bare metal. AgentScope 2.0 introduces production-grade Workspace and Sandbox support.

The framework abstracts “what the agent does” from “where it runs.” With a single line of configuration, you can force all agent-generated code to execute inside an isolated local Docker container, or seamlessly route it to an E2B cloud sandbox.

Architecture Diagram

What This Means for Your Backend Workflow

If you are architecting backends for enterprise scale or physical hardware applications, AgentScope 2.0 alters your entire deployment strategy.

1. Native Cloud & Kubernetes Deployment: Unlike experimental wrappers, the agentscope-runtime package is built for the cloud. It natively supports horizontal scaling via Kubernetes, detached background processes, and managed cloud deployments with auto-recovery and health checks.

2. Built-in Reinforcement Fine-Tuning: This is the framework’s “killer feature.” AgentScope integrates directly with the Trinity-RFT library. Because the framework logs all agent execution traces perfectly through the MsgHub, you can run reinforcement learning directly on those logs to fine-tune the underlying LLM. Alibaba demonstrated this by pushing a math-solving agent’s accuracy from a baseline 75% up to 85% purely through framework-driven fine-tuning.

3. Tooling via MCP: AgentScope natively supports the Model Context Protocol (MCP). You can seamlessly plug your agents into any MCP-compatible server (databases, local file systems, or custom API endpoints) using a centralized allowlist, ensuring your agents only touch the data they are authorized to see.

Try It Now

AgentScope 2.0 is open-source and ready for local testing. You can set up a secure, sandboxed agent in just a few lines of Python.

Bash

pip install agentscope
# For Kubernetes deployment capabilities:
pip install "agentscope-runtime[ext]>=1.0.0"

Python

from agentscope.agent import Agent
from agentscope.tool import Toolkit, Bash, Read, Write
from agentscope.model import DashScopeChatModel
import asyncio

# Setup the agent with a constrained, secure toolkit
agent = Agent(
    name="System Architect",
    system_prompt="You are a production engineer. Analyze the logs securely.",
    model=DashScopeChatModel(model="qwen3.6-plus"),
    toolkit=Toolkit(tools=[Bash(), Read(), Write()])
)

# Start the asynchronous event stream
async def main():
    # The MsgHub handles explicit state passing under the hood
    async for event in agent.reply_stream("Analyze the latest Docker crash logs."):
        print(event)

asyncio.run(main())

If you are tired of debugging silent prompt failures and want to build AI systems with the same architectural rigor as your traditional microservices, AgentScope 2.0 is the framework you have been waiting for.

Follow for more deep-dives on AI engineering, agent frameworks, and the infrastructure of the post-GPT era.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: The Robin Hood of AI: How HuggingFace’s upskill Steals Genius from Big Models and Gives It to the Small Ones
subtitle: What if you could bottle up Claude Opus’s problem-solving process and inject it into a model that ru
excerpt: What if you could bottle up Claude Opus’s problem-solving process and inject it into a model that runs on your laptop — for a fraction of the cost?
contentHtml:
What if you could bottle up Claude Opus’s problem-solving process and inject it into a model that runs on your laptop — for a fraction of the cost?

There’s a quiet revolution happening in how we build AI agents — and it doesn’t involve training a bigger model. It involves something far more elegant: teaching.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

HuggingFace just dropped a blog post that should be in every ML engineer’s bookmarks. It’s about a tool called upskill, a pipeline for extracting domain expertise from a powerful frontier model and packaging it into a transferable skill file — one that lets weaker, cheaper, and even fully local models punch way above their weight class.

Let’s unpack what’s going on, why it matters, and how you can use it today.

The Problem: Specialized Knowledge Is Expensive

Here’s the dirty truth about AI agents in production: the tasks that actually matter — writing GPU kernels, navigating your internal codebase, generating domain-specific configurations — are exactly the tasks where base models struggle the most.

Your options have historically been:

  • Pay more → Use GPT-4 or Claude Opus on every call (expensive, slow, API-dependent).

  • Fine-tune → Collect data, pay for training, manage model versions (complex and brittle).

  • Write longer prompts → Stuff documentation into every context window (wasteful, hits token limits).

upskill proposes a fourth path: distil the reasoning process, not the weights.

What Are Agent Skills?

Before we get to upskill, let’s define the primitive it builds on: agent skills.

An agent skill is devastatingly simple. It’s just a directory with a SKILL.md file — a markdown document that captures the how-to for a specialized task: the conventions, the gotchas, the step-by-step logic. Think of it as a domain expert’s mental model, written down and injected into the agent’s context window on demand.

What makes them powerful is their portability. Whether you’re using Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, or a local llama.cpp server, agent skills slot in the same way:

Plaintext

{agent}/skills/{skill_name}/SKILL.md

They’re not model-specific. They’re not framework-specific. They’re just files. And that simplicity is the whole point.

The upskill Pipeline: A 3-Act Play

Here’s the beautiful part: the upskill tool automates the process of creating and evaluating these skills. The workflow has three acts.

Act 1 — Let the Expert Work

You start with a powerful model — Claude Opus 4.5 in HuggingFace’s experiment — and give it a hard, domain-specific task. In their case: write optimized CUDA kernels for the H100 GPU using HuggingFace’s kernel-builder library.

The key here is letting the model struggle a little. The naive attempts, the corrections, the documentation lookups — all of this ends up in the agent trace: a raw log of every step the model took to solve the problem.

This trace is your gold. Don’t discard it.

Act 2 — Compress the Genius into a Skill

Now you feed that trace back into upskill generate. The tool does something clever: it reads the trace, identifies the key patterns, decisions, and domain conventions, and compresses them into a compact SKILL.md — typically around 500 tokens.

Bash

# From a task description
upskill generate "build optimized CUDA kernels for PyTorch using HuggingFace kernel-builder"

# Or from an existing agent trace
upskill generate "write kernels" --from ./trace.md

# Or iterating on an existing skill
upskill generate "add more edge case handling" --from ./skills/kernel-builder-cuda-kernels/

upskill also auto-generates test cases from the trace — input/output pairs that verify whether a model actually understands the task:

JSON

{
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": "Create a build.toml for a CUDA kernel targeting H100",
      "expected": {"contains": "9.0"}
    },
    {
      "input": "Write a basic CUDA kernel template with proper includes",
      "expected": {"contains": "cuda_runtime.h"}
    }
  ]
}

Act 3 — Transfer and Benchmark

Here’s where the Robin Hood analogy earns its name. You take the skill you built with an expensive model and test it on smaller ones:

Bash

# Evaluate across multiple models
upskill eval ./skills/kernel-builder-cuda-kernels/ \
    --model haiku \
    --model kimi \
    --runs 5

# Evaluate on a fully local model via llama.cpp
upskill eval ./skills/my-skill/ \
    --model "unsloth/GLM-4.7-Flash-GGUF:Q4_0" \
    --base-url http://localhost:8080/v1

The results from HuggingFace’s own experiment were striking:

  • Kimi-K2-Thinking went from ~40% accuracy to 85% with the skill — a 45-point jump.

  • Token usage dropped significantly for some models, meaning the skill isn’t just improving quality; it’s improving efficiency.

  • Claude Opus 4.5 itself showed no improvement (and higher token usage) — correctly signaling you shouldn’t add a skill for a model that doesn’t need it.

That last point is important. upskill isn’t snake oil. It gives you data.

The CUDA Kernel Case Study: What’s Actually In The Skill?

The generated SKILL.md for H100 CUDA kernels isn’t generic advice. It encodes the kind of specificity that would take hours to gather from documentation:

  • H100 uses compute capability 9.0 (not 8.0, not 8.6 — 9.0).

  • Shared memory should be aligned to 128 bytes.

  • Async memory copies require __CUDA_ARCH__ >= 900.

  • Project structure must follow the kernel-builder conventions with build.toml, kernel_src/, and torch-ext/.

All of this, roughly 500 tokens, loads into context on demand. You can now tell a cheap model:

“Build a fused LayerNorm + GELU kernel optimized for H100.”

And get a correct, compilable result — because the skill tells it exactly what correct looks like.

Architecture Diagram

Two Dimensions of Performance (Most People Miss the Second One)

The HuggingFace team deserves credit for tracking both dimensions of model performance:

1. Accuracy — Does the model produce correct outputs with the skill vs. without it?

2. Token efficiency — Does the model need fewer tokens to reach the same quality?

This matters enormously at production scale. Even if a model achieves the same accuracy either way, a skill that cuts token usage in half halves your API bill on every call. For Kimi-K2-Thinking, the skill delivered improvements on both axes. That’s the sweet spot.

What This Means for Your Stack

If you’re building production agents, upskill introduces a new design pattern worth internalizing:

The Knowledge Extraction Loop:

  1. Identify your hardest, most repetitive domain-specific tasks.

  2. Solve them once with your best model and export the trace.

  3. Run upskill generate to compress the expertise.

  4. Run upskill eval across your candidate models (including local ones).

  5. Pick the cheapest model that meets your accuracy bar.

  6. Ship the skill alongside your agent config. Done.

This is especially powerful for:

  • Internal tooling — your codebase conventions, your API schemas, your deployment config formats.

  • Domain expertise — legal clause structures, medical coding patterns, financial formula conventions.

  • Tribal knowledge — the stuff that lives in senior devs’ heads and never makes it to docs.

The Honest Caveats

The tool is young. Community testers have reported brittle eval behavior on Windows, path resolution quirks, and token usage spikes on some runs. The upskill eval command in particular has some rough edges. The team is actively iterating, but go in with realistic expectations for an early-stage OSS tool.

Also: not every skill transfers well to every model. upskill eval will tell you where the improvement is real and where it isn’t. Trust the data, not the hype.

Try It Now

Bash

pip install upskill

export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

# Generate a skill from a task description
upskill generate "write nvidia kernels"

# Or from an existing trace
upskill generate "write nvidia kernels" --from ./trace.md

# Evaluate on multiple models
upskill eval ./skills/my-skill/ --model haiku --model sonnet

The HuggingFace Upskill repo is open source. The Agent Skills specification is public. The H100 kernel-builder skill is already live on the Hub.

The knowledge is already packaged. All you have to do is steal it — responsibly.

Follow for more deep-dives on AI engineering, agent frameworks, and the infrastructure of the post-GPT era.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: The Architecture of Autonomy: Deconstructing Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5
subtitle: Generative AI API calls have traditionally been stateless and instantaneous.
excerpt: Generative AI API calls have traditionally been stateless and instantaneous.
contentHtml:
Generative AI API calls have traditionally been stateless and instantaneous. You send a prompt, you wait a few seconds, and you get a token stream back. But what happens when you need a system to run autonomously for days, dispatching sub-agents, writing code, and verifying its own logic against complex diagrams?

On June 9, 2026, Anthropic released Claude Fable 5, introducing their highly anticipated “Mythos-class” architecture to the public. Unlike traditional models, Fable 5 is not designed for simple chatbot interactions; it is an autonomous orchestrator built for long-horizon knowledge work.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

For system architects and engineers building multi-layered ML backends, Fable 5 represents a paradigm shift. It changes how we design API interactions, forcing a move from blocking HTTP requests to asynchronous, stateful memory loops. Let’s dive into the backend engineering of Fable 5, the mathematics of its dynamic safety routing, and how to architect your systems to handle it.
The Deep Dive: The Anatomy of a Mythos-Class Backend

Traditional LLMs fail at multi-day tasks because they suffer from context degradation and lack an internal framework for delegation. Fable 5 solves this through three distinct backend engineering breakthroughs: Asynchronous Sub-Agent Orchestration, Persistent Markdown Memory, and Dynamic Safety Routing.

1. Asynchronous Sub-Agent Dispatch

Rather than trying to process a massive, multi-step problem sequentially in a single 1M-token context window, Fable 5 acts as an orchestrator. When faced with a complex task (like auditing a codebase or analyzing dense financial architectures), the main model dispatches parallel sub-agents.

Crucially, the communication between the orchestrator and sub-agents is asynchronous. The main loop does not block while waiting for a sub-agent to finish a web search or code compilation; it continues managing other active threads.

2. The Dynamic Safety Classifier (Fallback Routing)

Because Fable 5 possesses elite capabilities in software engineering and logic, Anthropic implemented an aggressive, real-time safety architecture. Instead of simply refusing a harmful prompt, the system uses a dynamic routing mechanism.

When a payload enters the Fable 5 API, it first passes through a suite of safety classifiers trained specifically on offensive cybersecurity and biochemical threats. If a query crosses a specific risk threshold, the API dynamically freezes the Fable 5 inference and quietly routes the prompt to an older, safer model—Claude Opus 4.8.

The Mathematics of Dynamic Fallback

We can model this routing architecture as a piecewise function relying on a probabilistic risk classifier. Let x represent the input data payload and C_{safety}(x)$represent the ensemble classifier computing the probability of a critical policy violation.

The backend routing mechanism R(x) operates as:

Where $\tau$ is the strict safety threshold set by Anthropic. This architectural choice is brilliant for system uptime: instead of throwing a hard HTTP 400 error that breaks automated pipelines, the API gracefully degrades to a secure fallback model, preserving the workflow without crashing the agent harness.

The Architecture Block Diagram

Here is the system flow for a Fable-driven agentic harness. Notice how the safety layer intercepts the request before it ever touches the Mythos-class compute.

The System Design Link: Engineering for Fable 5

If you are integrating Claude Fable 5 into your cloud infrastructure (whether directly or via AWS Bedrock), you must re-engineer your application backend.

  1. Move to Event-Driven Webhooks: Because Fable 5 runs tasks that can take hours or days, your backend cannot hold an open REST connection. You must implement webhook architectures or asynchronous polling loops to catch the output when the orchestrator finally resolves the task.

  2. Build Explicit Memory Banks: Anthropic notes that Fable 5 thrives when it has a dedicated system to read and write state. You should architect a fast NoSQL database (like Firebase) or a simple file-storage system where Fable 5 can dump Markdown files summarizing its “lessons learned” from previous runs. The model uses these external states to overcome context degradation.

  3. Data Retention Compliance: Be aware of infrastructure requirements. Anthropic requires a 30-day data retention policy for all traffic on Mythos-class models to detect sophisticated abuse patterns. Your cloud architecture must securely log these payloads to comply with the API requirements.

By shifting your architecture from stateless text-generation to stateful, multi-agent orchestration, you can harness Fable 5 to automate end-to-end engineering tasks that previously required entire teams of human developers.

The TL;DR

  • The Problem: Traditional LLMs fail at multi-day, complex tasks because they lack asynchronous delegation and suffer from context degradation.

  • The Solution: Claude Fable 5 acts as an autonomous orchestrator, dispatching parallel sub-agents and using persistent file systems to track long-horizon goals.

  • The Architecture: Fable 5 utilizes dynamic safety routing. If real-time classifiers detect high-risk cybersecurity payloads, the system seamlessly falls back to Claude Opus 4.8 to deliver a safe response without breaking the API pipeline.

  • The System Impact: Integrating Fable 5 requires shifting your backend from blocking REST APIs to event-driven Webhooks with dedicated NoSQL memory states.
