title: Vision at the Edge: Deconstructing the NMS-Free Breakthrough for Drone Payloads
subtitle: When deploying computer vision on autonomous drones—a challenge anyone who has optimized payloads in
excerpt: When deploying computer vision on autonomous drones—a challenge anyone who has optimized payloads in a drone boot camp knows intimately—you are fighting a constant war against two enemies: thermal throttling and latency.
contentHtml:
When deploying computer vision on autonomous drones—a challenge anyone who has optimized payloads in a drone boot camp knows intimately—you are fighting a constant war against two enemies: thermal throttling and latency.

For years, the gold standard for real-time vision has been the YOLO (You Only Look Once) family of models. They are incredibly fast at generating bounding boxes around objects. However, up until recently, every YOLO model suffered from a hidden, glaring flaw that system architects despised: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS). NMS is a post-processing step. When a model looks at an image, it doesn’t just draw one box around a target; it draws dozens of overlapping boxes. NMS is the algorithm that forces the CPU to sift through all those boxes and delete the duplicates. It is slow, it scales terribly with complex scenes, and it drains battery power on edge devices.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Recently, researchers achieved a massive breakthrough by completely eliminating NMS from the architecture. Let’s unpack the mathematics of Consistent Dual Assignments, look at the block architecture, and explore exactly why this changes the game for edge electronics.

The Deep Dive: The NMS Bottleneck and Bipartite Matching

To understand the solution, we have to look at how traditional object detectors are trained. They use one-to-many assignment. For a single ground-truth object in an image, the network is rewarded for generating multiple positive predictions. During inference, this creates a chaotic cluster of bounding boxes that must be cleaned up by NMS.

The breakthrough architecture utilizes a Dual-Label Assignment strategy during training. It pairs the traditional one-to-many branch with a new one-to-one branch.

In the one-to-one branch, the network is forced to select the single best bounding box for an object. It does this using a mathematical concept called Bipartite Matching.

The Mathematics of the Loss Function

During training, the system calculates the matching cost between the network’s predictions and the actual ground truth. Let $y$ be the ground truth objects and $\hat{y}$ be the network’s predictions.

The matching cost function $C$ for a single prediction $i$ involves a classification loss and a bounding box regression loss:

Where:

  • pi is the predicted probability score.

  • ci is the true class label.

  • bi and ^bi are the predicted and true bounding box coordinates.

The network uses the Hungarian Algorithm to find the optimal assignment permutation $\hat{\sigma}$ that minimizes the total cost across all predictions:

Because the one-to-one branch is strictly penalized for duplicate boxes, it learns to naturally suppress redundancies.

Here is the brilliant engineering trick: Both branches are optimized together during training, but the one-to-many branch is physically deleted from the model before deployment. The model retains the rich feature learning of the heavy branch, but deploys with the lean, NMS-free architecture.

The Architecture Block Diagram

Here is the system flow of the dual-assignment architecture. Notice how the heavy post-processing is completely bypassed during live inference.

The Hardware Link: Why Edge Engineers Should Care

If you are writing C++ firmware to process camera feeds on a drone, or analyzing sensor data on an industrial VMC machine during automated quality inspections, eliminating NMS drastically changes your system design constraints.

  1. Deterministic Latency: NMS creates variable latency. If a camera sees a blank wall, NMS takes 0 milliseconds. If it sees a dense crowd of 100 people, NMS latency spikes, potentially causing a drone’s flight controller to miss a critical control loop. By removing NMS, the inference time becomes perfectly constant and deterministic.

  2. CPU Offloading: On edge systems (like Jetson Nanos or embedded TPUs), the neural network runs on the GPU/Accelerator, but NMS forces the data back onto the fragile, low-power CPU. Eliminating NMS keeps the entire pipeline contained within the AI accelerator.

  3. Thermal Management: Constant data transfer between the GPU and CPU generates heat. In a sealed payload with no active cooling, thermal throttling is fatal. End-to-end inference without post-processing drastically reduces the thermal envelope.

By shifting the complexity entirely into the training phase, we can deploy models that operate at sub-millisecond latencies, fundamentally expanding what autonomous hardware can achieve in the physical world.

The TL;DR

  • The Problem: Traditional object detection models rely on Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS), a computationally heavy post-processing step that causes latency spikes on edge hardware.

  • The Solution: New architectures utilize “Consistent Dual Assignments,” training a one-to-one branch using bipartite matching to naturally prevent duplicate bounding boxes.

  • The Math: The Hungarian algorithm forces the network to minimize a matching cost function, isolating the single most accurate prediction for every object.

  • The Hardware Impact: Dropping NMS allows for deterministic latency, zero CPU bottlenecking, and superior thermal management for drone payloads and embedded electronics.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

title: Beyond the Token: Why JEPA is the Blueprint for Physical AI
subtitle: Generative models like LLMs and diffusion architectures have dominated the artificial intelligence l
excerpt: Generative models like LLMs and diffusion architectures have dominated the artificial intelligence landscape for the past three years.
contentHtml:
Generative models like LLMs and diffusion architectures have dominated the artificial intelligence landscape for the past three years. They are spectacular at generating text and images, but they harbor a fundamental flaw when applied to real-world, physical systems: they waste immense computational energy predicting irrelevant details.

Unlike text generation, physical environments—whether monitoring real-time automotive intrusion detection systems or processing continuous time-series data from medical sensors—do not operate in neat, discrete tokens. If an autonomous drone is navigating a street, it does not need to predict the exact pixel texture of the leaves on a distant tree. It only needs to understand the abstract concept: there is a tree, and it is moving in the wind.

Thanks for reading Paramveer's Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

This is where Yann LeCun’s Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) comes in. JEPA shifts the paradigm away from generative reconstruction and toward predictive representation. For system architects and engineers building at the intersection of AI and hardware, JEPA is not just a new model—it is a pathway to deploying real-time, low-latency AI on the edge.

The Deep Dive: How JEPA Rewires Machine Learning

To understand why JEPA is revolutionary, we must look at how it breaks away from standard generative architectures.

Traditional generative models (like autoencoders or autoregressive LLMs) try to reconstruct the missing parts of an input in the raw data space. This forces the model to dedicate heavy compute to high-frequency, unpredictable noise. JEPA bypasses the raw data space entirely. Instead of predicting pixels or tokens, it predicts the abstract representation of the missing data within a learned latent space.

The Architecture Block Diagram

Here is the conceptual flow of a standard Image-JEPA (I-JEPA) system:

The Mathematics of Latent Prediction

In a JEPA system, the objective is to make the predicted representation ŝᵧ as close to the actual target representation sᵧ as possible.

Let x be the context (what the model sees) and y be the target (what the model needs to predict).

Step 1 — The context encoder extracts a representation:

sₓ = Eᵩ(x)

Step 2 — The target encoder extracts a representation:

sᵧ = Eᶿ(y)

Step 3 — The predictor neural network takes sₓ and an optional latent variable z (which handles uncertainty) to output a prediction:

ŝᵧ = Pᵩ(sₓ, z)

The system is trained by minimizing the distance between the predicted representation and the actual target representation. The loss function ℒ is expressed using a squared L2 norm:

ℒ = 𝔼ₓ,ᵧ [ ‖ Pᵩ(Eᵩ(x), z) − Eᶿ(y) ‖²₂ ]

Because the loss is calculated in the latent space rather than the pixel space, the model learns robust, semantic features of the world without memorizing noise.

To prevent representation collapse (where the encoders simply output zero for everything), JEPA updates the target encoder weights θ using an exponential moving average (EMA) of the context encoder weights φ, rather than through direct gradient descent.

The Hardware Link: Why Edge Engineers Should Care

Why does predicting latent variables matter for physical systems? It comes down to latency, power consumption, and hardware constraints.

When building machine learning pipelines for microcontrollers, drone payloads, or integrating ML into precision manufacturing and VMC workflows, memory bandwidth and power draw are your primary bottlenecks.

1. Lower Compute Overhead

Because JEPA does not reconstruct high-resolution images, the decoder/predictor network is significantly smaller and faster than a diffusion model or a heavy generative transformer.

2. Robustness to Sensor Noise

Physical hardware is messy. Camera sensors pick up thermal noise, motion blur, and glare. Because JEPA learns to ignore low-level pixel noise and focus on high-level semantics, it is inherently more resilient to the noisy data streams generated by real-world electronics.

3. Efficient Real-Time Inference

In a deployed system, you can strip away the target encoder. The system only runs the context encoder and the predictor, instantly generating semantic understanding of the environment with sub-millisecond latency.

If you are designing the system architecture for high-speed robotics or continuous data pipelines, shifting from a generative paradigm to a predictive, joint-embedding architecture is the key to operating safely within strict power budgets.

The TL;DR

The Problem — Generative AI wastes immense compute predicting irrelevant, low-level details (like exact pixels), making it inefficient for real-time physical systems.

The Solution — JEPA (Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture) predicts missing information in an abstract, mathematical latent space rather than the raw data space.

The Math — The system minimizes the distance between predicted representations and actual representations, using an exponential moving average to update weights and prevent collapse.

The Hardware Impact — JEPA enables highly efficient, low-latency computer vision that is perfectly suited for edge devices, constrained microcontrollers, and autonomous robotics.
